hero-lede: You keep hearing the word agent everywhere, and it sounds technical and a little scary. Here is the good news: it really isn't. By the time you reach the end of this page, you'll have built one all by yourself — with no code and no jargon — simply by playing with the pieces and seeing what each one does. Just drag, tap, and watch it come together.

s1-p1: An agent is simply an AI that you've handed some standing instructions to. You write down, just once, who it should be and how it ought to help — and from that moment on it behaves that way every single time, for anyone who comes along to talk to it. That really is the whole idea: a little worker that you describe using nothing but words.

An agent is just an AI you've given standing instructions to. You write down, once, who it should be and how it should help — and from then on it behaves that way every time, for anyone who talks to it. That's really the whole idea: a little worker you describe in words.

An agent is an AI you've given standing instructions: write down once who it is and how it helps, and it behaves that way every time, for anyone. That's it — a little worker you describe in words.
An agent: an AI with standing instructions — written once, applied every time, for anyone. A worker you describe in words.
s1-p2: It isn't a robot, it isn't a piece of code, and it isn't magic of any kind. If you're able to describe a job to a brand-new colleague using plain everyday English, then you can write an agent. The only real skill is learning which of your words actually carry the weight — so let's start by getting a feel for that.

It isn't a robot, it isn't code, and it isn't magic. If you can describe a job to a new colleague in plain English, you can write an agent. The only skill is learning which words actually matter — so let's feel that first.

s2-p1: Here's the secret that nobody bothers to tell beginners: the AI is the exact same one for everybody. The reason one person gets back something brilliant while another gets back mush comes down to one simple thing — what they actually told it. Watch this: we'll take a vague request and add one piece at a time:

s3-p1: That birthday request was a one-off — you asked it once and you got back a single answer. An agent is exactly the same trick, except it's written down just once and pointed at a whole job instead of a single question — and it ends up talking to total strangers, not only to you. Because of that, it needs a couple of extra pieces: the facts it has to stick to, and the limits on what it's actually allowed to promise. Let's go ahead and build a real one.

A one-off: asked once, answered once. An agent is that, written once, aimed at a job, for strangers — so add the facts it follows and the limits it can promise. Build one.

s5-recap:
• Who it is — the role you give it to play.
  • Its job — what it helps people with (and what it should leave well alone).
  • The facts it has to follow — your policy, your prices, your truth.
  • Its limits — the things it must never promise or do.
  • When it isn't sure — say so plainly, and pass it to a person.
  • Its tone — warm, plain, and brief.
  • The answer shape — what the reply should look like.

  • The answer shape — how the reply should look.
